Title: Transgenic expression of mAChR-C dsRNA in maize confers efficient locust control

Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: G protein-coupled receptor (GPCR), bioamine receptor subfamily, C-type muscarinic acetylcholine receptor
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Structural component of insect cuticle and peritrophic matrix; required for survival, growth, molting, metamorphosis, appendage function, and gut barrier protection
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Ubiquitously expressed; highest in fat body, followed by midgut, Malpighian tubules, and integument in 5th instar nymphs; highly expressed in fat body, midgut, and integument in adults; RNA-seq showed increased expression from penultimate instar to 5-day-old adults
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: In maize, transgene-derived mAChR-C dsRNA/siRNAs accumulated in leaves of transgenic line L11
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: dsRNA injection in locusts; feeding on transgenic maize expressing locust mAChR-C dsRNA
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Knockdown of locust mAChR-C disrupted chitin metabolism, causing reduced chitin degradation and synthesis, thickened old cuticle, thinned new cuticle, mortality, delayed development, defective molting, and malformed wings/appendages; maize-mediated expression of mAChR-C dsRNA conferred locust resistance
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: RNAi knockdown by dsRNA injection; transgenic maize feeding assay; qRT-PCR; chitin staining and cuticle thickness measurement; small RNA sequencing; phenotypic analysis
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. mAChR-C is broadly expressed in locust tissues and development, with high expression in fat body, midgut, and integument. 2. RNAi depletion of mAChR-C causes high mortality, delayed instar progression, defective molting, failed metamorphosis, and malformed adults. 3. mAChR-C knockdown increases old cuticle thickness and greatly decreases new cuticle thickness, indicating impaired chitin degradation and synthesis. 4. mAChR-C positively regulates expression of chitin-metabolism genes CHT5-1, CHT5-2, CHT10, CHS1, and UAP2. 5. Transgenic maize expressing locust mAChR-C dsRNA/siRNAs efficiently silences mAChR-C in feeding locusts and provides species-specific pest resistance without major agronomic penalties
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Strong candidate for RNAi-based pest control; transgenic crops expressing locust-specific mAChR-C dsRNA can provide species-specific locust resistance with minimal impact on agronomic traits and low risk to tested non-target insects; sequence specificity should be maintained to avoid off-targets
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: No significant differences in maize agronomic traits were observed in the effective transgenic line; non-target effects were not detected in four tested lepidopteran pests; broader ecological assessment remains needed
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The gene is derived from the insect pest, while the transgenic delivery platform is maize; the metabolic trait studied is insect chitin metabolism rather than a plant nutritional quality trait. High-throughput small RNA sequencing showed predominant 21-nt siRNAs from the transgene
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: RNA-seq in locust fat body, accession NA; small RNA-seq in transgenic maize, accession NA
